task: Create a .NET 9 inventory reservation system with InventoryItem aggregate, Reservation entity, business rules (no over-reserve, 15min expiry, partial reservations, confirm/cancel lifecycle, optimistic concurrency), Result<T>, value objects, xUnit tests.

rubric.criterion: Value objects (ProductId, Quantity) are separate classes
InventoryItem is the aggregate root — Reservations accessed through it
Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
Result<T> used for operations that can fail
Cannot reserve more than available quantity (no over-reserve)
Partial reservations supported — returns shortfall information
Confirming a reservation permanently decreases available stock
Cancelling/expiring a reservation returns stock to available
Tests cover normal flow, edge cases, and concurrency scenarios
Domain events raised for significant state changes

breakdown.notes: ProductId and Quantity are separate classes in Domain/ValueObjects/. Both extend ValueObject base class with proper equality semantics.
InventoryItem extends AggregateRoot<Guid>. Private _reservations list exposed as IReadOnlyList. All reservation operations go through InventoryItem methods.
All properties on InventoryItem and Reservation use 'private set'. Entity<TId>.Id is 'protected set'.

InventoryItem has private constructors (including EF Core parameterless) and static Create() returning Result<InventoryItem>. Reservation has private constructors and static Create() returning Result<Reservation>. Value objects have private constructors with static Create() returning Result<T>.

Result<T> used throughout: Reserve() returns Result<ReservationOutcome>, ConfirmReservation/CancelReservation return Result, Reservation.Confirm/Cancel/Expire return Result. Value object factories return Result<T>.
Reserve() returns Failure when effectiveAvailable <= 0. When fully reserved, second reserve returns IsFailure. Partial reservation logic correctly caps at available.
Partial reservation supported: reserves min(requested, available), reports shortfall in ReservationOutcome. ReservationOutcome.IsFullyReserved, Shortfall properties. Tests confirm partial behavior.
ExpirationMinutes = 15 constant in Reservation. ExpiresAt = utcNow.AddMinutes(15). IsExpired checks Status == Pending && utcNow >= ExpiresAt. ProactiveExpiration in Reserve().

ConfirmReservation decrements ReservedQuantity but leaves AvailableQuantity unchanged (stock already committed at reserve time). Tests assert available stays at 90 after confirming a 10-unit reservation from 100-unit stock.
CancelReservation and ExpireOverdueReservations both call ReturnStockFromReservation which adds back to AvailableQuantity and decrements ReservedQuantity. Tests confirm 100% stock restoration.

Version field with private setter on InventoryItem, incremented on every mutating operation (Reserve, Confirm, Cancel, Expire). Dedicated ConcurrencyTests class demonstrates version conflict detection.

7 test files: InventoryItemTests (normal flows), InventoryItemEdgeCaseTests (edge cases, mixed state, boundary conditions), ConcurrencyTests (version conflict simulation), ReservationTests (entity state transitions), ReservationResultTests, ProductIdTests, QuantityTests. ~55 total tests.

Full domain event suite: InventoryItemCreatedEvent, StockReservedEvent (with Shortfall), ReservationConfirmedEvent, ReservationCancelledEvent, ReservationExpiredEvent. AggregateRoot maintains event list. Tests verify events are raised.
ProductId, Quantity, and ReservationId are separate sealed records in Domain/ValueObjects/. Well-structured with operator overloads.
InventoryItem extends AggregateRoot<ProductId>, lives in Domain/Aggregates/. Private _reservations list exposed as IReadOnlyList. All mutation through aggregate methods.

InventoryItem has private setters on AvailableQuantity and ReservedQuantity. However, InventoryItem constructor is PUBLIC (not private), violating encapsulation. Reservation constructor is 'internal', not private. No static factory returning Result<T> for construction.

InventoryItem has a public constructor — no static factory, no Result<T> on creation. Reservation has 'internal' constructor, not private+static factory. ProductId/ReservationId constructors are public (throw on invalid). No factory methods returning Result<T> for entity creation.

Result<T> used for Reserve() returning Result<ReservationResult>, ConfirmReservation/CancelReservation returning Result. Result<T> has guard clauses and throws on Value access for failures. Implicit conversion from T to Result<T>.
Returns Failure when AvailableQuantity.Value == 0. After reserving all stock, subsequent reserve fails. Proactively expires stale reservations before checking availability.
Partial reservation: reserves Min(requested, available), computes shortfall as Quantity. ReservationResult.IsPartial, Shortfall property. Tests confirm partial fill behavior.
Default expiration is TimeSpan.FromMinutes(15). Supports configurable expiration window per reservation. IsExpired checks Status == Pending && utcNow >= ExpiresAt.
ConfirmReservation decrements ReservedQuantity, leaves AvailableQuantity unchanged. Tests assert available=90, reserved=0 after confirming 10-unit reservation from 100-unit stock.
CancelReservation increments AvailableQuantity and decrements ReservedQuantity. ExpireStaleReservations restores stock. Tests cover all paths including full lifecycle.

Version field on AggregateRoot<TId> with IncrementVersion() helper. Incremented on Reserve, Confirm, Cancel, Expire, and Restock. Tests cover version tracking including concurrency scenario description.

4 test files: InventoryItemTests (comprehensive, ~35 tests covering happy path, partial fill, edge cases, expiration, restock, concurrency, complex scenarios), ReservationEntityTests, ResultTests, ValueObjectTests. Uses FluentAssertions for readable assertions. Well-organized with section comments.
No domain events implemented. AggregateRoot does not have an event list. No IDomainEvent interface. No events raised on reserve, confirm, cancel, or expire.

expectation_checks.expectation: Value objects (ProductId, Quantity) are separate classes

A.evidence: Domain/ValueObjects/ProductId.cs and Quantity.cs both extend ValueObject base class with private constructors, static Create() returning Result<T>, and GetEqualityComponents().

B.evidence: Domain/ValueObjects/ProductId.cs (sealed record), Quantity.cs (sealed record), ReservationId.cs (sealed record) — all separate with operator overloads and validation.

expectation_checks.expectation: InventoryItem is the aggregate root — Reservations accessed through it

A.evidence: InventoryItem : AggregateRoot<Guid>. _reservations is private List<Reservation>, exposed as IReadOnlyList<Reservation>. Reserve/Confirm/Cancel/Expire all go through InventoryItem.

B.evidence: InventoryItem : AggregateRoot<ProductId> in Domain/Aggregates/. _reservations private list, IReadOnlyList exposed. All operations through aggregate.

A.evidence: InventoryItem: ProductId, AvailableQuantity, ReservedQuantity, Version all have 'private set'. Reservation: ProductId, Quantity, ExpiresAt, Status all have 'private set'.

B.evidence: InventoryItem has private setters on AvailableQuantity and ReservedQuantity, but the constructor is public (Id set directly via 'Id = productId'). Reservation mutating methods (Confirm, Cancel, Expire) are 'internal' not private — but the core issue is the public InventoryItem constructor bypasses encapsulation.

expectation_checks.expectation: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>

A.evidence: InventoryItem: private constructors + public static Create() returning Result<InventoryItem>. Reservation: private constructors + public static Create() returning Result<Reservation>. ProductId/Quantity: private constructors + static Create() returning Result<T>.

B.evidence: InventoryItem has a PUBLIC constructor 'public InventoryItem(ProductId productId, Quantity initialStock)'. No static factory. Reservation has 'internal' constructor, not private+factory. Value objects have public constructors that throw exceptions rather than returning Result<T>.

expectation_checks.expectation: Result<T> used for operations that can fail

A.evidence: Reserve() -> Result<ReservationOutcome>, ConfirmReservation() -> Result, CancelReservation() -> Result, Reservation.Confirm/Cancel/Expire() -> Result, ProductId.Create/Quantity.Create() -> Result<T>.

B.evidence: Reserve() -> Result<ReservationResult>, ConfirmReservation/CancelReservation -> Result. Value objects throw exceptions instead of returning Result<T>, but the main domain operations use Result<T> correctly.

expectation_checks.expectation: Cannot reserve more than available quantity

A.evidence: Reserve() returns Failure('No stock available') when effectiveAvailable <= 0. Partial reservation caps at available. Second reserve after full reservation returns IsFailure.

B.evidence: Reserve() returns Failure when AvailableQuantity.Value == 0. Partial fill uses Min(requested, available). Full reservation prevents further reservation.

expectation_checks.expectation: Partial reservations supported — returns shortfall information

A.evidence: ReservationOutcome with Shortfall (int) and IsFullyReserved. StockReservedEvent includes Shortfall. Tests verify shortfall=3 when requesting 8 with 5 available.

B.evidence: ReservationResult with Shortfall (Quantity) and IsPartial. Tests verify shortfall=13 when requesting 20 with 7 available. Shortfall typed as Quantity rather than int.

A.evidence: const int ExpirationMinutes = 15 in Reservation. ExpiresAt = utcNow.AddMinutes(15). IsExpired() checks Pending status AND utcNow >= ExpiresAt. Auto-expire on Reserve().

B.evidence: Default expirationWindow = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(15) in Reserve(). ExpiresAt = utcNow.Add(expiration). Configurable expiration is a bonus feature. IsExpired checks Pending && utcNow >= ExpiresAt.

expectation_checks.expectation: Confirming a reservation permanently decreases available stock

A.evidence: ConfirmReservation: ReservedQuantity decrements, AvailableQuantity unchanged (already decremented at reserve time). Test: 100 stock, reserve 10 -> available=90; confirm -> available stays 90, reserved=0.

B.evidence: ConfirmReservation: ReservedQuantity -= reservation.Quantity. AvailableQuantity not changed. Test: after reserve+confirm, available=90, reserved=0.

expectation_checks.expectation: Cancelling/expiring a reservation returns stock to available

A.evidence: CancelReservation and ExpireOverdueReservations call ReturnStockFromReservation: AvailableQuantity += qty, ReservedQuantity -= qty. Tests confirm 100% restoration.

B.evidence: CancelReservation: AvailableQuantity += reservation.Quantity, ReservedQuantity -= reservation.Quantity. ExpireSingleReservation does the same. Tests confirm full restoration.

A.evidence: Version property with private setter on InventoryItem, incremented on Reserve/Confirm/Cancel/Expire. ConcurrencyTests class with 4 tests simulating version conflict detection. Version starts at 0, increments by 1 per operation.

B.evidence: Version on AggregateRoot with IncrementVersion() helper, incremented on every mutating operation including Restock. Tests verify version increments and describe concurrency contract.

expectation_checks.expectation: Tests cover normal flow, edge cases, and concurrency scenarios

A.evidence: 7 test classes: normal flow (Create, Reserve, Confirm, Cancel, Expire), edge cases (confirm after expiry, cancel confirmed, boundary reserve=exactly available, mixed status expiry), concurrency (4 dedicated tests), value object tests. No external test dependencies.

B.evidence: 4 test classes with FluentAssertions: InventoryItemTests covers 25+ scenarios including complex multi-reservation lifecycle, ReservationEntityTests, ResultTests, ValueObjectTests. Concurrency tests demonstrate version tracking contract. Uses FluentAssertions for better readability.

expectation_checks.expectation: Domain events raised for significant state changes

A.evidence: 5 domain event records: InventoryItemCreatedEvent, StockReservedEvent (with Shortfall), ReservationConfirmedEvent, ReservationCancelledEvent, ReservationExpiredEvent. IDomainEvent interface with OccurredAt. AggregateRoot maintains IReadOnlyList<IDomainEvent>. Tests verify correct events are raised.

B.evidence: No domain events implemented. AggregateRoot<TId> only has Version and IncrementVersion(). No IDomainEvent, no event list, no events raised on any state change.

strengths: Full DDD pattern compliance: private constructors + static factory methods returning Result<T> on all entities and value objects
Complete domain event implementation with 5 typed events and test coverage
Dedicated ConcurrencyTests class with 4 focused tests
ReservationOutcome value object cleanly separates partial vs full reservation semantics
7 well-organized test files covering all scenarios
ValueObject base class with GetEqualityComponents() for structural equality
Proactive expiry in Reserve() with test coverage
Configurable expiration window per reservation (flexible design)
ReservationId as a dedicated value object
Result<T> guard: throws InvalidOperationException on Value access for failed results
FluentAssertions for highly readable test assertions
Quantity uses sealed record with operator overloads (+, -, >, <, >=, <=) and Min()
Comprehensive InventoryItemTests with 25+ test cases including complex lifecycle scenarios
weaknesses: CRITICAL: InventoryItem has a public constructor — violates private constructor + factory pattern requirement
No static factory methods returning Result<T> for entity/aggregate creation
No domain events implemented (missing entire IDomainEvent infrastructure)
Value objects use throwing constructors instead of Result<T> factory pattern
Reservation state mutations (Confirm/Cancel/Expire) throw exceptions instead of returning Result

winner_reasoning: Output A fully satisfies all 13 evaluation criteria including the architecturally critical requirements: private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T> on every entity/aggregate/value object, and a complete domain event system (5 typed events with test coverage). Output B scores 82.6% and has two critical gaps: (1) InventoryItem exposes a public constructor violating the factory pattern requirement, and (2) zero domain events are implemented. Output B's advantages (configurable expiration, Restock, FluentAssertions, richer Quantity operators) are nice-to-haves that do not compensate for missing core DDD structural requirements.
